items.evidence_text:
Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I feel stretched—like I’m doing “fine” on paper, but the debt payments and monthly bills are just… heavy.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep this practical. Let’s start with income at a high level—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client: Total gross is 89800 a year.
Advisor: Okay. And is that mostly salary, or is it a mix?
Client: It’s a mix—salary plus business income, and some bonus. There’s also a little rental income.

Client: It’s a mix—salary plus business income, and some bonus. There’s also a little rental income.
Advisor: Helpful. We’ll break it down in a minute, but I’ll anchor on the total for now. On the spending side, what are your total monthly expenses—everything, not just debt?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 7100 a month.
Advisor: Thanks. When you say expenses, does that include the mortgage and credit card payments, or is that separate in your head?
Client: I kind of lump it all together sometimes, but yes, that 7100 includes the debt payments.

Client: I kind of lump it all together sometimes, but yes, that 7100 includes the debt payments.
Advisor: Perfect—then let’s isolate the debt piece. Roughly what are you paying per month toward debts total?
Client: Total monthly debt payments are 4500.
Advisor: Okay—so that’s the mortgage plus the credit card minimums or whatever you’re sending there.
Client: Yeah. Mortgage is the big one, and then I’m paying on the card every month too.

Client: Yeah. Mortgage is the big one, and then I’m paying on the card every month too.
Advisor: Understood. Now, switching gears to what you’ve built up—about how much do you have in investable assets, like brokerage, retirement, and cash accounts?
Client: Investable assets total 85150.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m not missing anything—does that include your taxable brokerage, your 401(k) or IRA-type account, and your bank cash?
Client: Yes, those three, plus I have a smaller private investment thing through an insurance company.

Client: Loans outstanding total is 61500.
Advisor: Okay. And just to tie the home into this—what do you estimate the property value is right now?
Client: Property value is 56950.
Advisor: Thanks. Switching briefly back to the income mix you mentioned earlier—can you walk me through the pieces, even if it’s a little messy?
Client: Sure. I have business income of 21250 a year, and salary of 18550 a year.

Advisor: Yeah, I hear you. One sec, I’m just noting those down.
Advisor: So if we add those balances together, your total loans outstanding across mortgage plus card—what number do you use for that total?
Client: Loans outstanding total is 61500.
Advisor: Okay. And just to tie the home into this—what do you estimate the property value is right now?
Client: Property value is 56950.

Advisor: Okay, Margaret—let’s zoom in on the debts, because that’s what’s making the month-to-month feel tight.
Advisor: Starting with the mortgage: what’s the current balance outstanding?
Client: The mortgage balance outstanding is 39500.
Advisor: Got it. And what’s the monthly payment you’re making on that mortgage?
Client: It’s 3950 a month.

Client: Final payment date is 2046-12-01.
Advisor: Great. Now the other big one you mentioned—the credit card. What’s the balance on that right now?
Client: The credit card balance outstanding is 22000.
Advisor: And what are you typically paying per month toward the card?
Client: 600 a month.

Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I feel stretched—like I’m doing “fine” on paper, but the debt payments and monthly bills are just… heavy.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep this practical. Let’s start with income at a high level—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client: Total gross is 89800 a year.
Advisor: Okay. And is that mostly salary, or is it a mix?
Client: It’s a mix—salary plus business income, and some bonus. There’s also a little rental income.

Advisor: Understood. Now, switching gears to what you’ve built up—about how much do you have in investable assets, like brokerage, retirement, and cash accounts?
Client: Investable assets total 85150.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m not missing anything—does that include your taxable brokerage, your 401(k) or IRA-type account, and your bank cash?
Client: Yes, those three, plus I have a smaller private investment thing through an insurance company.
Advisor: Got it. And you also mentioned you own a home—so we’ll treat that separately from investable assets.

Advisor: Got it. And what state are you living in right now?
Client: NY.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, shifting from the numbers to how you want to invest—when you think about risk, where do you land? Conservative, moderate, aggressive…?
Client: I’m moderate. Like, I can handle some ups and downs, but with the credit card at 23.96% I’ve been acting more cautious lately.
Advisor: Totally fair. When you say moderate, do you mean you’re okay with a balanced mix—some stock exposure, some safer stuff—so long as we’ve got a plan for the debt?

Client: Yes, moderate is the right word for it.
Advisor: Great. Last one in this set—do you know what tax bracket you’re generally landing in? Even a band is fine.
Client: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. And just so I’m connecting the dots: you’ve got the mortgage at 5.93% with the final payment date 2046-12-01, and the credit card at 23.96%, plus the accounts you listed—taxable brokerage 42600, retirement 22650, bank cash 10050, and that private markets piece 9800—and the property value 56950.
Client: Yeah, that’s the picture. It’s a lot, but yes.

Client: Thank you—yeah, one thing at a time.
Advisor: Okay, Margaret—thanks. Before we wrap today, I just want to capture a couple household basics so everything lines up.
Advisor: First, what’s your marital status right now—single, married, partnered?
Client: I’m single.
Advisor: Got it. And in the household, is it just you—how many adults live in the household?

Client: It’s a mix—salary plus business income, and some bonus. There’s also a little rental income.
Advisor: Helpful. We’ll break it down in a minute, but I’ll anchor on the total for now. On the spending side, what are your total monthly expenses—everything, not just debt?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 7100 a month.
Advisor: Thanks. When you say expenses, does that include the mortgage and credit card payments, or is that separate in your head?
Client: I kind of lump it all together sometimes, but yes, that 7100 includes the debt payments.

Advisor: Got it. And in the household, is it just you—how many adults live in the household?
Client: Just me—1 adult.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one’s more about how we’ll work together. You’re engaging us through our RIA—registered investment advisor—channel, correct?
Client: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great, thank you. And country-wise, you’re based in the US, right?

Advisor: Hi Margaret—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what made you decide to come in now?
Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I feel stretched—like I’m doing “fine” on paper, but the debt payments and monthly bills are just… heavy.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep this practical. Let’s start with income at a high level—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client: Total gross is 89800 a year.

Advisor: Okay—debt plan first. That makes sense, Margaret.
Advisor: Before we build the payoff steps, I want to make sure I’ve got your work situation right, because it affects how aggressive we can be month to month. Are you currently employed?
Client: Yes, I’m employed.
Advisor: Got it. And in your household setup—just to be clear—are you the primary person making the financial decisions?

Client: No, nothing planned. It’s busy, but stable.
Advisor: Okay—so staying with the debt plan for a second, Margaret. I’ve got the mortgage and the credit card as the two big ones. Before I start suggesting payoff steps, what’s your role at work—like broadly, what kind of position are you in?
Client: I’m in an exec role—exec.
Advisor: Got it—executive level. Thanks. And just so I’m not making assumptions, is that like a corporate executive role versus, say, self-employed full-time?
Client: Corporate. I do have business income too, but my occupation group is exec.

Client: Property value is 56950.
Advisor: Thanks. Switching briefly back to the income mix you mentioned earlier—can you walk me through the pieces, even if it’s a little messy?
Client: Sure. I have business income of 21250 a year, and salary of 18550 a year.
Advisor: Okay—keep going.
Client: There’s also bonus income of 9900 a year, and another business income stream of 36700 a year.
